title: Context Layering
concept: Organizing context into ordered layers: system rules, persistent memory, retrieved facts, conversation, current query.

exercises.prompt: Before we dive in: when you give an AI assistant instructions, extra facts, AND a question all at once, what do you think happens if they're just thrown together with no order?

options.text: The AI always perfectly sorts it out with no issues
The AI might miss instructions or misprioritize the question
The AI refuses to respond entirely

exercises.explain: When context is unorganized, models can lose track of which parts are rules versus facts versus the actual question — leading to answers that ignore instructions or miss the point. That's exactly why layering matters!

cards.title: Why Does Order Matter?
cards.body: Imagine handing someone a messy pile of sticky notes with rules, facts, and questions all jumbled together. They'd struggle to know what's most important. LLMs face the same problem — so we organize their context into clear, ordered layers.

cards.title: What Is Context Layering?
cards.body: Context layering means organizing everything you feed an LLM into a deliberate order: system rules first, then persistent memory, then retrieved facts, then conversation history, then the current query last. Each layer has a job, and the order helps the model know what matters most.

cards.title: It's Like Building a Sandwich
cards.body: A club sandwich has a bottom bun, meat, lettuce, cheese, and top bun — always in that order, or it falls apart. Context layers work the same way: each layer sits in its spot, and together they hold up the whole response.

cards.title: A Shopping Assistant's Context
cards.body: Layer 1 (system): 'Only recommend in-stock items.' Layer 2 (memory): 'User is vegetarian.' Layer 3 (retrieved): 'Today's stock list.' Layer 4 (conversation): 'User asked about pasta earlier.' Layer 5 (query): 'What sauce goes well?' Stacked in order, the model gives a smart, safe, personalized answer.

cards.title: Myth: Order Doesn't Matter
cards.body: Myth: 'As long as all the info is in the prompt somewhere, the model will use it correctly.' Truth: LLMs weigh position and structure. Mixing rules with chit-chat or burying the current question can cause the model to ignore instructions or answer the wrong thing.

cards.title: Layered vs. Jumbled Context
cards.body: A layered context window is like a well-organized binder with labeled tabs — the model can quickly find what it needs. A jumbled one is like a shoebox of loose papers — technically all the info is there, but it's hard to use reliably.

left.text: Rules → Memory → Facts → Conversation → Query, each in its own clear section

right.text: Rules, chat, facts, and question all mixed randomly with no structure

exercises.prompt: Which layer typically comes last in a well-structured context window?
exercises.explain: The current query goes last because it's the freshest, most immediate thing the model needs to respond to — everything before it exists to help the model answer it well.

exercises.prompt: True or False: Persistent memory (like long-term user preferences) should come before retrieved facts but after system rules in a layered context window.
exercises.explain: True! The typical order is: system rules → persistent memory → retrieved facts → conversation → current query. Memory (like 'user prefers vegetarian food') is stable and general, so it sits closer to the rules, while retrieved facts are more situation-specific and come next.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the blank with the correct layer name.
exercises.sentence: The system rules layer should typically be placed first, while the current query goes last.
exercises.explain: System rules go first because they set the boundaries and behavior for everything that follows — the model should see the 'ground rules' before anything else.

exercises.prompt: Which of these context structures was likely generated by an AI system with NO layering strategy (just dumped everything together)?

options.text: System: rules. Memory: user prefs. Retrieved: docs. History: chat. Query: question.
Rules, chat message 3, a fact, chat message 1, question, chat message 2, another fact — all mixed in one block with no headers
A prompt with clear sections labeled SYSTEM, MEMORY, FACTS, CONVERSATION, QUERY in that order

exercises.explain: Option B shows no organization at all — rules, facts, and messages are scrambled together with no separation or order, which is a telltale sign of an unstructured, non-layered context window.

exercises.prompt: You're building a customer-support AI. It needs: (1) rules like 'never share prices without approval', (2) the fact that this specific user is a 'Gold Tier' member (looked up from a database), (3) the last 3 messages of this chat, and (4) the user's brand-new question. How should these be layered in the context window?

options.text: Random order — the model figures it out regardless
System rules first, then retrieved facts (Gold Tier), then conversation history, then the current query last
Current query first so the model sees it immediately, rules last
Only include the current query — everything else is unnecessary

exercises.explain: The correct layering follows the standard order: system rules set the boundaries first, retrieved facts (like Gold Tier status) ground the response in real data, conversation history gives recent context, and the current query comes last since it's what the model must respond to right now. Skipping layers (like dropping rules or facts) risks broken behavior or wrong answers — every layer plays a distinct, necessary role.
